subject: Human Resources
title: Frederick Taylor and Scientific Management
description: A 60-second lesson on Frederick Taylor and Scientific Management.

hook.text: In 1911, Frederick Taylor published a book arguing that every job could be studied, measured, and optimized.

buildup.text: Scientific management broke jobs into precise steps, timed each one, and eliminated 'wasted' movement.

discovery.text: Taylor's methods dramatically increased productivity. Output per worker doubled or tripled in many factories.

twist.text: Workers hated it. They felt reduced to machines—monitored, timed, and stripped of autonomy and judgment.

climax.text: Taylorism created the field of industrial engineering—and the backlash created the need for employee relations.

punchline.text: Taylor optimized the work. HR had to save the workers.

quiz.question: What was the main criticism of Taylor's scientific management?
options: It treated workers like machines with no autonomy
It was too expensive to implement
